speaker: interviewer
text: Phone's off the hook?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you allow me-- My name is Christian Froelicher. We're in Elsternwick, Melbourne, Australia. It's the 9th of December, 1996. And the survivor is Samuel Friedman. What's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Samuel Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your only name always?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, during the German occupation, my name changed to Friedeman, and my first name to Waclav. Sounds more Polish than Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 29Th of November, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Place of birth, Kalisz. You want me to spell it? K-A-L-I-S-Z, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between large cities like Lodz and Poznan, just in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I'm 76.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maternal grandparents lived in the same town. I don't remember my grandmother. She died early. And-- but I remember very well my grandfather, who lived till 1940, and died in Warsaw ghetto, at the age of 80. He was an Orthodox man. He was running a hardware shop, time being. And later he was studying his books, [? he'd get mad at us, ?] and all these things. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He had so many grandchildrens that probably-- I don't-- I don't know if he knew my name. But we never talked to him or never had any closer association. My paternal grandparents lived in Lodz, which is the large city not far from Kalisz. My grandfather was running a jewelry shop, watchmaker and jeweler, on Piotrkowska Street in Lodz, 88. He was living nearby. Grandmother and grandfather was very friendly. And I used to come to Lodz often, [INAUDIBLE] from time to time. And I was very well welcomed. And we talked together. And they-- he never bothered to correct me. Because he was an Orthodox one. And I may have come to a meal without my head cover. But he never mentioned it. Only the grandmother showed me. Listen, grandfather is looking, so-- and this is all about I can say about the grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grand-- maternal grandfather, Eliahu. The grandmother, I don't remember. The same is with the grandparents in Lodz. Israel was his name. Her name, I don't know. Or I don't remember, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, I would say, a very good and friendly man. He was a family man. We never had any troubles from father or any problems which I remember. I don't remember. He was running his shop, which was in the center of the town, jewelry and watchmaking. And to us, the children, he was a-- I would say, a good father. We talked together. We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: We used to go in summer, early in the morning, to very long walks in the gardens. There are very nice gardens around Kalisz. And he didn't interfere with my studies or something especially. And what else I can say? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob, Yaakov.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Watchmaker. Jeweler, watchmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the business, again, it was-- I think it was a rather prosperous business in very tough times. The times were very, very tough. And I understand that money wasn't so much around. But still, I went to a private school. And they found the money to send me to a university in France. And I was getting an allowance, monthly allowance. So this was approximately the situation. In the shop, my father was working and selling jewelry and things. My mother was helping, almost everyday, I think. We are-- we were living nearby, as I showed you in the picture. And it was rather a happy family-- a happy family. My friends were coming to visit me, and I was going to my friends. I had a lot of friends. This was a Jewish gymnasium. You know, we were involved in all these Purims and Hanukkahs and things like this. So that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] What was the name of the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Polish? Polish? [POLISH] [POLISH]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You can make it. That's a good one. What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish gymnasium in Lodz, branch, Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, the same. Very loving person, taking care of the children, a good mother, mother cooking. You know, everybody remembers this. And I think she was a very good person, very good mother. She spoke perfectly, Polish. And think-- I think my Polish is from my mother. She went to school, to Russian school, and to German school and to Polish school. I don't know anymore. And then-- well, she was a caring person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-S-E. And this is in English, but Polish was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-Z-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Polish at home with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many siblings did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sister, 10 years my-- younger than myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugenia, or Genia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was a-- [LAUGHS] --very good sister, as much as I remember. But she was a child. She went to school. She was a very good pupil. She learned-- she studied at the same gymnasium, the same Jewish gymnasium. And we didn't associate very much because she was so-- so much younger. So I met her at home. And we were very friendly brother and sister. And I remember during beginning of the war, she was very sick. And I was running like mad to bring medicines and things like-- something to help her out. I don't want to bother to go into particulars. This is what I remember, this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your family house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The family house was a flat of three rooms. Not bedrooms, three rooms and a kitchen. We had nice furniture, which is still standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I checked this in 1988. It's impossible to move, you know? The furniture was so heavy then, built. They couldn't move it. It's still standing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had my room and my bed, my radio. And in the evening I was listening to the radio, to different stations, like Bucharest and Budapest and these-- the gypsy music and things. And in my room, I was going my homework. And friends were coming. And I was going to my friends, staying with friends, and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What heating arrangements did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, this was the oven. This was the tile oven. And we use-- we didn't use briquettes. We didn't have briquettes yet. But still, some kind of tinder and-- and coal. This was the heating in the house. But they are very, very good, comfortable. You don't forget, the climate in Poland was severe. Winters were very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cooking facility was, again, coal and tinder and wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the street address of your flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street address is-- no. The place of 11th of November, number 16. Yeah, 16, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your friends in your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, it was my cousin, which I have here on pictures, Arunia. She lost her father very early. And her mother was working hard to keep-- to earn for the rest of the family, two daughters. And she was many, many times, staying with us. So she was a very close cousin. And we have a lot of memories together. She is now living in Jerusalem with her husband and children, family. This was my closest-- closest friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I have friends from school. You want the names? [? Henek ?] [? Morretz, ?] a very talented young man, very good friend of mine. He was writing poetry. And he perished somewhere in Russia. [NAME]-- I mentioned the names-- who is probably still living somewhere in Poland. I couldn't contact him. I was-- we were walking--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because going back from school to home, this was no transport, no buses or something. It was just walking. So we are walking and talking. And this was the occasion that we talked so much to the-- to-- with our friends until we got-- get home. It was half an hour or 40 minutes or so on foot. So we had a lot to talk about. And one of my best friends was Tadek Moroco. This is his name. Tadek Moroco was attending another school, the Polish school. His parents couldn't afford to send him to the private school. But we were very good friends. We went walking together for hours, Saturdays, Sundays. Or any free time, he was coming to our place. And I was going to his place. And for a time, we were living, even, in the same house. He was living on the upper floor. I was living on the lower floor. So we met together. And of course, talking, I don't remember what we were talking about then. But as a young-- we used to go together to cinemas, to theaters. He got a bike. So I was trying to ride his bike. His brother, Pol, or Paul Moroco, was a solicitor, young solicitor. He had a young family, wife and a little boy, Peter. And he was the-- one of the most clever men. He moved out of the area three, four months before the war. And he went to Rovno in Eastern Poland, very close to the Russian border. And as soon-- very soon in '39, he moved from Rovno to Stalino in Soviet Russia. From Stalino, he moved further up. I don't know where. And he landed up in Canada. But he was the one clever man who knew exactly what's going to happen. Everybody else was stupid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I reckon 70,000, with the population-- Jewish population, maybe 25,000. But still, the figures are just my guess. I-- I don't know. I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many non-Jewish friends did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because friends we had only from school. And in school, we had only the Jewish-- Jewish students. So I didn't have any connection. When I was there we have only Jewish friends. Even those of my friends who went to different schools, like I mentioned, this Tadek Moroco, he didn't have Polish friends. Even in his class, he had Polish-- Polish students.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he always associated with us. I have, here, a friend, living here in Melbourne, [? Montag, ?] [? Elishek ?] [? Montag ?] who attended a Polish school. But he-- he always came to us, to associate with us, with our group.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never, never thought about it. You are asking the question. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm surprised myself, that I didn't have a Polish friend. What was Jewish about your appearance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish about my appearance? Nothing. Otherwise, I wouldn't be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you dress as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we dressed in the uniform. This-- there was this compulsory school uniform, always. And we dressed in the uniform. Nothing-- nothing special. And the uniform was a tunic with a number, school number. Our school number was 190. And the blue one was for the first six years. And the last two years-- it was an eight-year school, you know. Last two years, we had the red ones, red numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a typical day at the gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: A typical day was getting there at 8:00. And the shammash was ringing the bell. Sammash's name was [INAUDIBLE]. And everybody went to his class. And we waited for the teacher. And when the teacher get in, we stood up, greeting the teacher. And then we started our lessons the usual way. I must, this occasion, say how good and how professional were the teachers at our school. I realize now that they were highly-educated people. You don't get, probably, today in the schools, so highly, just out from teacher's college.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you about anti-Semitism and about the prospects of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't tell nothing. Because it wasn't a topic to talk about, this. We knew-- in '33, or around '33, I remember that the teacher of German-- because we studied German at school as a-- as a foreign language. And the teacher of German came and we didn't want to start the lessons. We were-- it's kind of boycott. So he explained to us-- because of this, what happened in Germany, Nazis and so and so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he explained to us, if you want to fight your enemy, know his language. Learn his language. Well, the-- the protest was short-lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we studied the language till the end, till the matriculation, the four years of German, which was very, very useful for me. Because later on, I used my German in Germany. I could communicate.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those years, in the '30s, leading up to the war, what did you read and hear about what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew everything-- everything, because we read some German newspapers. From Czechoslovakia, not from Germany. And then, in our newspapers, we had a free press. We knew everything was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: I even urged my father, why shouldn't we go and to live in Palestine? But because it was in the '30s and Palestine was, at this time, a very unsecure place. And the problems of transferring goods and money and families and all this, well, this was not on my head. I could have packed my bag and go to Palestine tomorrow. But my father couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother's sisters did. Three sisters-- not in the same time-- but they slowly moved to Palestine, [INAUDIBLE]. One, Sara, [INAUDIBLE], later, [INAUDIBLE], was one of the people who established Mishmar HaEmek. They came back from Palestine with her husband, [? Kubar ?] [INAUDIBLE], to mention the name. And they told us about-- they-- they told us about the Palestine, the life, and things on the kibbutz. And some songs we learned from them, which I still know. And later on, her sister, Runa, went to Palestine and Genia. All three of them, I found after the war in-- in Israel, already, established with families and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall one of those Zionist songs? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Of-- Zionist. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. This was one of the songs which really we learned from Sara, when she came back from Palestine to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it translate to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between hills and rocks, the train is running. The train is running. And from all the boys, only you are my loving boy, or something. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. What magazines and newspapers did you read at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magazines, I can't remember. Newspapers, probably the regular Polish newspapers, [? Courier, ?] [? Kharkovsky, ?] [INAUDIBLE], something like this. But well, I didn't read papers. Because I wasn't interested so much in-- in reading papers or in politics. We had our own problems in between youngsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about that paper from Czechoslovakia that you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This we-- this we read only to improve our German. Because this was in German. And for-- from some reason, I don't know why, it came from Czechoslovakia. I don't know why. You know, we could have had it from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some films that you saw in your childhood and youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we saw a lot of films. Well, it was cinema where you could see two pictures and live appearance of people, live appearance of theater or some kind of comedy sketches. And then there-- cinemas, two people on one ticket. You know, the-- the situation was to bring the people to the cinema, to make it as cheap as possible. What I saw-- of course, one-- all the great pictures which were at this time. You had like, Charlie Chaplin, the whole series of Charlie Chaplin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we called them Flip and Flap, the fat and the thin. Stan Laurel, Oliver Hardy. Rings a bell? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stan Laurel and Oliver Hardy, the picture of them. And then we saw a lot of other pictures, Polish pictures and French. And we saw a-- I can't recall really, something which is outstanding. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because I can't remember the name of the pictures. I remember that we saw very many. We went many times to the-- to the cinema to saw-- to see pictures. But I don't recall the names. It's-- it's irrelevant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did it cost to get in?

speaker: interviewee
text: $0.49. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is 49 grosz. Where would you get the money from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, this is a good question. I got it from my father, always. But it wasn't so easy. I had to ask, and I had to wait. And I had to do my-- my-- my schoolwork first, and things like this. Eventually, I was getting the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about vacations you may have had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went-- we used to go for a vacation to a small farm, which was run by my uncle on behalf of the Jewish National Fund, [INAUDIBLE], or some other Jewish-- Zionist organization. The farm was specially preparing the [INAUDIBLE] people for migrating to Israel. My uncle was a huge man riding a black horse. And his-- his voice was heard everywhere in the farm. And we came in June-- probably June, July, for two months-- with my cousin, which I mentioned before. She didn't have her father. She lost her father early, Arunia. And my other sisters-- my-- my mother's sisters with children, we used to come to this place every year for two months, for many, many years. I can't remember how many year, but many years. And one of the pictures I have here, with the three children, is made in this place. Then we had vacations together with parents. We went somewhere, to a village or farm, to enjoy the fresh air. But we didn't have any long trip somewhere. Or I went to Lodz, to my grandparents, to spend with them, some-- a week or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about religious life in your hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you have to divide the population, the religious and the not so religious. You mean Jewish population? Well, the Orthodox, there was many synagogues in [INAUDIBLE], you know what I mean. Holidays, everybody was in the shul, my father, my mother, sister, and everybody. And we stayed as-- as-- as long as-- as long as the service was going on. Then we went home. And we had the usual meals, if this is Hanukkah and Purim, or Seder Pesach, of course. This is not so religious. But still, we observed all the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the religious-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --oh, how do you say it-- events. We have to change the tape.
